summary: Jeff Atwood presents Jerry Weinberg's Ten Commandments of Egoless Programming from the 1971 book 'The Psychology of Computer Programming.' The commandments focus on separating personal identity from code, accepting criticism gracefully, respecting colleagues at all skill levels, and embracing collaboration over solo heroics. Atwood highlights how these principles remain relevant decades later, emphasizing that the human aspects of software development are timeless. The post serves as a reminder that technical excellence must be paired with interpersonal maturity.
key_points: Separate your identity from your code—criticism of code is not criticism of you as a person
Seek input from others proactively, especially when you think you don't need it
Treat less experienced people with respect to avoid reinforcing negative stereotypes about developers
Authority comes from knowledge, not position or title
Embrace change as a challenge rather than fighting it as an inconvenience
money_quotes: No matter how much 'karate' you know, someone else will always know more.
The only true authority stems from knowledge, not from position.
Fight for what you believe, but gracefully accept defeat.
Don't be the guy coding in the dark office emerging only to buy cola.
Critique code instead of people – be kind to the coder, not to the code.
Nontechnical people who deal with developers on a regular basis almost universally hold the opinion that we are prima donnas at best and crybabies at worst.
The human principles of software are truly timeless.
key_insight: The human and interpersonal aspects of programming—humility, collaboration, and emotional maturity—are timeless principles that matter as much as technical skill.
